summary: I was born and raised in Riverside, California, where I also finished junior college. Growing up there, I helped my father at his restaurant after school until about 9 pm every day. Riverside was where I experienced both the challenges of being Japanese American and the community dynamics, including neighbors who were both friendly and hostile.

When I first started school, I only spoke Japanese, which made my early grades quite poor until about fifth grade. Over time, I lost most of my Japanese as I learned English. Although I wanted to teach languages, I did not plan to teach Japanese specifically, but rather any foreign language I could.

In my elementary school, most of my classmates were white, but there were a few Mexicans as well. However, in the lower grades, there were no Mexican students. I noticed that Mexican children attended the same schools as white children, unlike African Americans who had a separate school.

In my neighborhood, African American children had to attend a special school separate from the white and Mexican children. Most of them lived in a particular district and were not integrated with us. This segregation was a clear example of the racial divisions at the time.

In high school, I used to drive an old Ford Model T. One of my friends would come wait for me and we would go to school and come home together. She was sometimes ashamed to be seen in my old Model T, but I never minded because we were good friends.

I graduated from high school on the honor roll, which was quite strict at the time. Although I was in the honorable mention category, I had about 65% A's. The honor roll recognized the top ten students, and I was ranked 17th, but I was proud of my academic achievements despite the challenges.

I studied Spanish for four years in high school as part of my language education. Along with other languages, Spanish was one of the foreign languages I wanted to teach. This background helped prepare me for my goal of becoming a language teacher.

In high school, I took three years of Latin in addition to other languages. This was part of my broad language education, which also included English, Spanish, and later French. I hoped to use this knowledge to become a language teacher.

During my two years at junior college, I studied French alongside English. This continued my pursuit of language education, which I was passionate about despite my parents' concerns about my future prospects. French was an important part of my language studies.

The Great Depression made life very hard for my family, especially my father who ran a restaurant. He had to let go of his cooks and my mother began cooking to help out. I also had to work hard while attending junior college because my family was struggling financially during that time.

My father bought a house in the names of John Hagen, myself, my sisters, and younger brothers to prove that an alien father could buy land in his children's names. This led to a legal battle that lasted about three years and went all the way to the California Supreme Court. The case was finally won in 1919, affirming our right to own property.

The California Supreme Court was where my father's land ownership case was decided in 1919. After three years of fighting, the court ruled that an alien father could buy land in the names of his children. This was a significant victory for our family and for Japanese Americans at the time.

On Thanksgiving Day, my mother would send a turkey dinner to a civil war veteran's widow who lived next door. We were probably the only family who did that, showing kindness despite the difficulties we faced. It was a way for us to maintain good relations with some of our neighbors.

My mother came from a Samurai family in Japan, which was part of her background before she married my father. This heritage was significant to her, and it contrasted with the new life she built here in America. It was a proud part of our family history.

My father joined the United States Navy after deciding to leave Japan. He served on a Coast Guard Cutter that patrolled the Pacific Coast. His service was during the time of the Spanish–American War, and he hoped to gain citizenship through his military service, but unfortunately, he never did.

My father served on a Coast Guard Cutter that went up and down the Pacific Coast during his time in the Navy. This was part of his naval service around the time of the Spanish–American War. His experience on the cutter was significant, though later labor disputes prevented him from returning to the ship.

My father's naval service on the Coast Guard Cutter took him along the Pacific Ocean coast. This was during the late 1890s and early 1900s, a time when he was trying to establish himself in America. The Pacific Ocean was the backdrop of his early American experience.

San Francisco was where my father faced discrimination while serving in the Navy. When Japanese busboys and stewards took a day off, they were not allowed back on the ship due to pressure from striking African American stewards. This incident contributed to my father losing his naval position.

My father served in the United States Navy during the time of the Spanish–American War around 1898. He wanted to be recognized as a veteran of that war to gain citizenship but was told he was short by just 13 days of service. This was a source of frustration for him throughout his life.

My father sought citizenship of the United States through his naval service but was ultimately denied because he was just short of the required service time. Despite this, he never gave up on wanting to be a citizen and believed he deserved it. This struggle reflected the broader challenges faced by Japanese immigrants at the time.

The Riverside Inn was a famous local establishment run by Frank Miller, a man who supported my father during his legal battle over land ownership. Although Frank Miller himself could not help financially, he referred my father to his brother Ed Miller, who encouraged him to fight the case. This support was crucial in our family's fight for property rights.

Frank Miller, the hotelier who ran the Riverside Inn, was an important figure in my father's fight for land ownership. While he could not provide financial help, he directed my father to his brother Ed Miller, who offered encouragement and support. Frank Miller's influence helped my father find the resources to pursue the case.
Edison Uno was not mentioned in my story, so I have no personal experiences or memories related to him.
Kenjinkai was not part of my personal narrative, so I do not have any direct experiences or stories involving this organization.
